coredata.dc:title: Understanding the role of pressurized CO2 in the direct recycling process of Li-ion battery positive electrode
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Hayagan, Neil
Guillou, Pierre
Olchowka, Jacob
Ercicek, Fatma
Lecoutre, Carole
Nguyen, Olivier
Aymonier, Cyril
Marre, Samuel
Erriguible, Arnaud
Philippot, Gilles
coredata.dc:description: The ternary mixture of carbon dioxide (CO2), triethyl phosphate (TEP), and acetone enabled an efficient delamination of Li-ion battery (LIB) positive electrode inciting an interest to study the ternary mixture behavior. Pressurized fluids, such as CO2, are known to be beneficial in various chemical processes. However, the behavior of CO2, when mixed with TEP and acetone is not well understood, particularly under pressure and temperature conditions. This study investigates the behavior of CO2 or nitrogen (N2) in mixtures with TEP and acetone at various compositions, using experimental investigations of the ternary system. Experimental data covers four temperatures at 35°C, 70°C, 100°C and 120°C at a constant pressure of 100 bar. The phase behaviors of the binary and ternary mixtures were observed using a transparent reactor, while the compositions were analyzed in situ with Raman spectroscopy. Under isobaric conditions, a single phase was observed with CO2 at 35°C, both in the binary systems with either TEP or acetone, as well as in the ternary mixture. In contrast, a biphasic system was observed at higher temperatures (70°C, 100°C, and 120°C) in all mixtures containing CO2. Specifically, the biphasic condition at 55°C at 100 bar, of the mixtures were semi-quantitatively investigated using Raman spectroscopy to probe the compositions in the vapor and liquid phases. These observations elucidate the crucial role of CO2 in the delamination of the positive electrode in LIB using the TEP-acetone-CO2 system enabling high efficiency, low solvent consumption, and a faster processing time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pressurized CO2 technology
Phase behavior
CO2-TEP-acetone ternary mixture
CO2-TEP binary mixture
CO2-acetone binary mixture
Direct recycling of Li-ion batteries

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Phase behavior of the ternary mixture CO2 + TEP + Acetone
Probing the solubility of CO2 in the TEP + Acetone mixture using Raman spectroscopy
Impact of temperature on the solubility of CO2 and N2 in the Acetone- TEP mixture
Impact of temperature, CO2 (N2) and acetone on the viscosity of TEP

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: As part of the DESTINY PhD Programme, the authors acknowledge the funding from the European Union's Horizon2020 research and innovation programme under the Marie Sk\u0142odowska-Curie Actions COFUND (Grant Agreement # 945357 ), co-funded by Region Nouvelle Aquitaine (Project Region AAPR2021A-2020-11998810 ), from ANR through the STORE-EX Labex project ANR-10-LABX-76-01 , and the REGENERATE JCJC project ANR-22-CE05-0004 . This study also received financial support from the French government in the framework of the University of Bordeaux's IdEx \"Investments for the Future\" program / GPR PPM. We also acknowledge the CNRS.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Reactor set-up for the VLE experiments: 1. CO2/N2 cylinder, 2. Gas inlet valve, 3. ISCO high pressure pump, 4. pressurized gas valve, 5. Three-way valve, 6. Manometer, 7. Reactor inlet valve, 8. Oven with glass door (viewing and imaging purposes), 9 Transparent HP reactor, 10. Temperature display, and 11. Raman probe.

Physical observation of the fluid’s behavior inside a vertical sapphire tube at different temperatures in isobaric condition of 100 bar for (0.13xTEP + 0.87 yAcetone) initial composition + CO2 mixture where a) 30°C (1 bar) with a zoomed version in a-1), b) 120°C, c) 100°C, d) 70°C, and e) 35°C with their respective zoomed version.

Fermi diads of pure CO2 with Raman spectroscopy a) in different phases and b) superimposed of obtained data (★) on the digitized graph of upper and lower bands ratio of the Fermi diads as a function of density, reproduced with permission from AIP Publishing.

Observed spectra of fluids using Raman spectroscopy with a 100 bar at a) 35°C where a monophasic system is observed in binary and b) ternary systems, while b) and c) data are from 55°C the liquid and vapor phase, respectively.
Representative Raman spectra from Fig. 4 highlighting the Fermi diad for CO2 at 100 bar in the ternary mixture of TEP-acetone-CO2 at a) 35°C monophasic, b) 55°C liquid phase, and c) 55°C vapor phase.

Ternary phase diagrams highlighting the bubble curves of the system TEP, Acetone, and a) CO2 or b) N2 at 35°C, 70°C, 100°C and 120°C and at 100 bar. The three dashed-lines are the path of CO2 or N2 addition from the initial concentration of TEP and Acetone.
Viscosity evolution of TEP with a) either CO2 or N2, b) with acetone and CO2 and c) with acetone and N2 in different proportions from 35°C to 120°C at a constant pressure of 100 bar.
Specific information of the fluids involved in the study. Tc, Pc, and ω are critical temperature, critical pressure and the acentric factor.
Movement of the meniscus height(final height – initial height, cm) for the different mixtures at different temperatures pressurized by CO2 at 100 bar (Figures S1- S5 for CO2 system).
Movement of the meniscus height(final height – initial height, cm) for the different mixtures at different temperatures pressurized by N2 at 100 bar (Figures S6- S10 for N2 system).
Intensity ratio of the Fermi diad of pure CO2 (ʋ+ /ʋ -) in the aforementioned conditions and the density of CO2 in different conditions obtained from NIST [26].

head.ce:title: Understanding the role of pressurized CO2 in the direct recycling process of Li-ion battery positive electrode

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Univ. Bordeaux, CNRS, Bordeaux INP, ICMCB

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ternary mixture of carbon dioxide (CO2), triethyl phosphate (TEP), and acetone enabled an efficient delamination of Li-ion battery (LIB) positive electrode inciting an interest to study the ternary mixture behavior. Pressurized fluids, such as CO2, are known to be beneficial in various chemical processes. However, the behavior of CO2, when mixed with TEP and acetone is not well understood, particularly under pressure and temperature conditions. This study investigates the behavior of CO2 or nitrogen (N2) in mixtures with TEP and acetone at various compositions, using experimental investigations of the ternary system. Experimental data covers four temperatures at 35°C, 70°C, 100°C and 120°C at a constant pressure of 100 bar. The phase behaviors of the binary and ternary mixtures were observed using a transparent reactor, while the compositions were analyzed in situ with Raman spectroscopy. Under isobaric conditions, a single phase was observed with CO2 at 35°C, both in the binary systems with either TEP or acetone, as well as in the ternary mixture. In contrast, a biphasic system was observed at higher temperatures (70°C, 100°C, and 120°C) in all mixtures containing CO2. Specifically, the biphasic condition at 55°C at 100 bar, of the mixtures were semi-quantitatively investigated using Raman spectroscopy to probe the compositions in the vapor and liquid phases. These observations elucidate the crucial role of CO2 in the delamination of the positive electrode in LIB using the TEP-acetone-CO2 system enabling high efficiency, low solvent consumption, and a faster processing time.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Complex materials such as multi-material composites and layered materials, are increasingly utilized in advanced technological application. One notable example is secondary or rechargeable batteries, famous for their role in the ecological transition [1]. Batteries power numerous devices making them portable and enabling remarkable innovations; a spectacular curated drone show suited for any events that surpass traditional fireworks displays or facilitating environmentally friendly transportation through electric vehicles are some examples. Lithium-ion batteries (LIB), in particular, offer these advantages. However, inside the battery is a cell, composed of intricate complex multi-materials. This intricate material is then targeted for recycling due to its economic value, primarily stemming from the presence of high-value metals, in addition to addressing environmental concerns and mitigating resource scarcity [1]. Nonetheless, a great challenge arises from the composite material layers within a battery’s electrode [2].

Pressurized fluids systems, such as the use of CO2 have been extensively used in different processes for multi-material recycling because it enables lower temperature processes and lower (toxic) solvent consumption against other conventional solvent processes [3,4], while maintaining an efficient delamination efficiency [5,6]. In the field of LIB recycling, this technology offers significant advantages. For instance, we recently demonstrated a method by incorporating acetone and CO2 alongside TEP for separating the complex layered materials of the positive electrode of a LIB [7]. The implementation of pressurized technology enabled 6.7x lower solvent consumption, elimination of stirring, and the recovery of all components without damage, hence, qualifying it to direct recycling. Bai, et al., (2021) [8] introduced TEP for the chemical delamination method applied to LIB positive electrodes recycling, by stirring in atmospheric conditions. Bai’s stirring process consumes 10:1 TEP-to-electrode in weight at 100°C for 1 h. This large amount of TEP has to be properly removed since it negatively affects the electrochemical performance of the recycled material in new batteries [9,10]. Besides, we reported that after the chemical delamination process, the binder such as polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) dissolved in TEP makes it highly viscous, which complicate the removal of TEP from the surface of the active material. Therefore, it becomes necessary to remove the TEP with dissolved PVDF from the surface of the LiNi0.6Mn0.2Co0.2O2 (NMC622) particles, hence the reduction or minimization of the viscosity of TEP and its removal becomes necessary. To resolve the viscosity issue of TEP containing dissolved PVDF, acetone and CO2 were added with the purpose to (i) decrease the viscosity of the TEP/PVDF solution and to (ii) wash out the TEP and to facilitate the removal of PVDF. This CO2 approach not only allows cleaning the surface of the NMC622 particles, crucial for their further electrochemical performance, but also allows the recovery of PVDF. This innovative method was able to separate and recover all the materials and the NMC622 exhibited the same electrochemical properties to that of the pristine material. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis presented in our paper confirms that the NMC622 surface is clean of TEP [7], suggesting that the new method not only efficiently delaminates but also maintains the integrity of the materials. Therefore, this advanced and efficient delamination process, along with the advantages of using CO2, is worth-investigating.

In this context, this paper unravels the influence of CO2 and acetone additions on the estimation of the viscosity of TEP and its subsequent impact on the delamination process of the LIB positive electrode. Here, we combine Raman spectroscopy and direct optical observation to identify the components in each phase, leading to the estimation of the CO2 quantity in the liquid phase and the subsequent changes in the viscosity of the mixture. As a benchmark, N2 was selected to assess the role of CO2. Studying the behavior of TEP, a non-referenced organic solvent, proved to be challenging due to the limited available data. Consequently, investigating fluid mechanisms under pressurized conditions across various temperatures presented inherent difficulties. Lastly, the data of the solubilities of CO2-acetone has been extensively studied [11–13], while there are almost no experiments and studies reporting CO2-TEP [14,15] mixtures or the ternary [TEP – Acetone – CO2] mixture. Besides, the high pressure and temperature phase diagram and solubility of this ternary mixture has not been studied to the best of the authors’ knowledge.

The vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) experiments were performed using a high pressure resistant transparent reactor of 9 mL, one-tenth of the volume (0.9 mL) is filled with a mixture of triethyl phosphate (C2H5)3PO4 (TEP) (≥ 99.8 %, Sigma Aldrich, USA) and acetone (CH3)2CO (≥ 99.9 %, Honeywell, France) in an Ar-filled glovebox. The reactor is a sapphire tube (ID: 8 mm, OD: 18 mm, length: 20 cm) equipped with plugs on both sides with Viton O-rings. Each plug integrates a standard port for fluidic connections. The plugs are maintained in place thanks to an aluminum clamp (see Fig. 1) allowing reaching pressure up to 300 bar and temperature up to 150°C. Table 1

The pressure was generated using a piston pump (Teledyne Isco, 100DM), with volume and pressure measurement display, which serves at injecting either CO2 or N2. The reactor is homogeneously heated inside an oven with a glass window, allowing a real-time observation of the phase behavior. A thermocouple is inserted inside the reactor to access in situ temperature information. The initial mixture is tightly closed and connected to the set-up for controlled temperature and pressure conditions. Before, filling the reactor with either CO2 or N2 fluids, the initial height measurement was taken at 30°C (1 bar), then the reactor is heated up to 120°C and CO2 (CO2 > 99.9995 %) or N2 (N2 > 99.9999 %) is added up to reach 100 bar. The set-up is left standing at these conditions for at least 2 h with the pump working in constant 100 bar pressure mode at 120°C. After which, the temperature is lowered down to 100°C, keeping the pressure constant. The procedure is repeated at 70°C and 35°C, with the same pressure of 100 bar. Hayagan et al., (2024) presented the mixture in %vol such as 75TEP:25Acetone, 50TEP:50Acetone, and 25TEP:75Acetone [7] which is written as 0.56 xTEP + 0.44 yAcetone + zCO2, and so on.

In this work, the Peng-Robinson Equation of State (PR-EOS) was adopted to represent the VLE estimation of the ternary mixture of CO2 (or N2)-TEP-Acetone system under various temperatures (120°C, 100°C, 70°C, 55°C, and 35°C) at a constant pressure of 100 bar.

Peng and Robinson proposed a cubic equation of state of the form:

To describe the mixtures with the PR-EOS, the quadratic Van der Waals one-fluid mixing rule was assumed. It states that pure substance parameters for mixtures

with Z and f, the compressibility factor, and the fugacity, respectively.

With the same set-up as presented in Fig. 1, the fluids used and their mixtures were studied using an optical probe (12 mm working distance, Tmax = 60°C) in contact with the tube and connected to a QE-Pro High-Performance Spectrometer (Ocean insights) with a remote EM shutter, with data acquisition and processing using the OceanView 2.0.14 software. Raman characterization aims to identify the changes in the fluid compositions at specified temperature and pressure. The spectrometer is equipped with a 532 nm Laser (Oxxius L1C-532, 100 mW) to record the spectra in the Raman shift range of 100–4000 cm−1 with acquisition time of 30 s scan speed and 30 accumulations with a resolution of approximately 6 cm−1.

The solubility of CO2 into the mixture of TEP and acetone at different temperatures was investigated and the subsequent changes in the viscosity of the system were calculated. N2 was used as a benchmark fluid to compare the behavior of CO2 inside the reactor during the delamination process as previously demonstrated [7].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phase behavior of the ternary mixture CO2 + TEP + Acetone
ce:section.ce:para: This section highlights the observation of the phase behavior involved at heated and pressurized conditions of mixtures. Fig. 2 presents an example of a ternary system of the (0.13 xTEP + 0.87 yAcetone) initial composition + CO2 at a constant pressure of 100 bar and different temperatures using the transparent reactor (see Figures S1- S5 for CO2 and Figures S6- S10 for N2, systems). It is worth noticing that the use of a vertical reactor with a high length-to-diameter (L/D) ratio supports in observing fluids’ behavior, especially when there is a phase separation. This phase separation is indicated by the presence of a meniscus, wherein the discrimination between a vapor (lighter) and a liquid (denser) phase is clear and distinct, as shown in Fig. 2 below. It enabled an easy and direct observation of the fluid phenomena.

Fig. 2a is an arbitrary baseline of data and observation, initially the tube is heated at 30°C at atmospheric pressure, wherein the meniscus is marked at 3.2 cm. When the reactor is heated at 120°C and at a pressure of 100 bar, the meniscus moved to a higher level of 4.9 cm Fig. 2b. This meniscus is stable and is clearly observed even at lower temperature at the same pressure of 100 bar such as at 100°C and 70°C as shown in Figs. 2c and 2d. All of these suggests that at these conditions, a biphasic system for the mixture (0.13 xTEP + 0.87 yAcetone) + zCO2 is present.

The appearance and the movement of this meniscus at higher level indicates the solubility of CO2 in the mixture TEP + acetone. As mentioned earlier CO2 is soluble in acetone [11–13] and in TEP [14,15] hence, their ternary mixture could have this characteristic solubility. This solubility is proven when the temperature is lowered down to 35°C while maintaining the 100 bar pressure (Fig. 2e), a single phase is observed for the mixture, correlating with solubility.

When the reactor was pressurized and heated, an observation that the height of the fluid’s meniscus increased.
Tables 2 and 3 summarizes the meniscus displacement when the mixture is pressurized with either CO2 or N2, respectively. It is clear that the height of the meniscus with CO2-pressurized fluids is always higher than that of N2, this is due to the higher solubility of CO2.

The increased in pressure (from atmospheric pressure as the baseline) to 100 bar caused a change in the fluid’s, as indicated by meniscus movement from its initial position. This rise in meniscus increased the liquid volume due to the dissolved gas. This dissolved gas alters the density of the mixture. However, the change in temperature at a constant pressure of 100 bar did not change the position of the meniscus. Exceptionally, for the CO2 system, a single phase or monophasic system is observed at 35°C with CO2 for all mixtures, which is not the case with N2. This means that the meniscus movement or the solubility response of the system is mainly driven by the applied pressure than by the temperature, and by the different molecule of gas used, CO2 against N2 in this case.

There is also a clear trend with the CO2-pressurized fluid systems that the height of the meniscus increases with acetone, but decreases with TEP. While for the N2-pressurized fluid systems almost a constant height of meniscus is observed. This indicates that the behavior of CO2 and N2 are totally different even at the same conditions, due to the innate characteristics of each molecule. According to Gjaldbaek & Anderson [18], the solubility of CO2 in acetone is higher than of N2 and could be due to electronic polarization which is 6.6 and 4.4 (values are in Bunsen absorption coefficient, a), respectively. Also, N2 is known to be inert and unreactive, while CO2 has a higher level of chemical affinity. It is well-established that the solubility of gases in typical solvents is higher at elevated pressures and lower temperatures compared to higher temperatures at the same pressure [19]. While the density of the fluids mixture is affected both by pressure and temperature, and the amount of fluids that solubilized in the liquid phase. Obviously, these data on the height of the meniscus alone cannot unravel the behavior of fluids mixtures. Therefore, Raman spectroscopy was used to understand this deeper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probing the solubility of CO2 in the TEP + Acetone mixture using Raman spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: A Raman spectroscopy analysis was conducted to investigate the solubility of CO2 in the TEP + Acetone mixture in the case of biphasic systems under various conditions. The spectroscopy setup can only be operated at temperatures below 60°C. With this, experiments were conducted at 55°C and at 100 bar (Figure S11), which also demonstrated the presence of the meniscus, this set-up parameters will be used to verify the observations in a biphasic ternary mixture.

Initially, to verify if the set-up could effectively track the behavior of fluids, while accounting for potential interferences (transparent tube diffraction, resolution of the spectrometer, etc.), the Raman spectra of pure CO2 in different phases: liquid CO2 (18°C; 100 bar); vapor CO2 (18°C; 50 bar); and supercritical CO2 (55°C; 100 bar) with densities of 0.870 g/cm3; 0.145 g/cm3, and 0.325 g/cm3, respectively were measured. Using the obtained spectra, we investigated the attribution of the Fermi diad bands of CO2 at 1265 cm−1 and 1370 cm−1 [20–24].

According to Garrabos, et al. [20] and Rosso & Bodnar [24], these two diads shifts to lower frequencies (wavenumber or Raman shift) with increasing density. The frequency shift of the lower band (ʋ-) has a higher density dependency compared to the upper band (ʋ+). Additionally, the ratio of the upper and lower bands (ʋ+/ʋ-) increases with increasing density. Indeed, the data obtained (see Table 4) corroborated to this description, 2.01, 1.78, and 1.51 for liqCO2, scCO2, and vapCO2, respectively and followed the trend, aligning with the data of Garrabos et al. [20] (see Fig. 3). This proves that the Raman set-up used with a low resolution is suitable for spectroscopy study, especially for CO2 and its Fermi diads. Brauer (2015) [25] mentioned that a “poor” spectral resolution set-up could be beneficial in Raman spectroscopy for pressurized fluids system while the variation of mixture composition can add peak shifting due to changes in intermolecular interactions, but these effects are not regarded, as the deconvolution uses pure compounds, therefore, low spectral resolution is sufficient. Although this is true, one can only rely on the big changes of the spectra of the mixture due to high pressure and high temperature conditions with such resolution of the spectrometer, meaning that small details are not detected and are not the objective of these investigations. As suggested by Fig. 3b, the set-up can now be used to profile the solubility of CO2, by tracking the attribution of the Fermi diad bands of CO2 at 1265 cm−1 and 1370 cm−1 [20–24].

For the investigated ternary mixtures, the CO stretch band from 1690 to 1740 cm−1 was used to monitor acetone [27,28]. The band at 1442 cm−1 was assigned to the asymmetric CH3 mode while the small hump at 1477 cm−1 was attributed to the CH2 bending mode, both characteristics of TEP [29]. Experiments were conducted at 35°C and 55°C and 100 bar for all concerned TEP-Acetone compositions.

Concerning the experiments at 35°C and 100 bar, the Fermi diad bands of CO2 were present and relatively intense in its binary mixtures with either acetone or TEP, as shown in Fig. 4a, and in their ternary mixtures, as shown in Fig. 4b. The bands of TEP and acetone were also detected. The presence of the band contributions from acetone, TEP, and CO2 in the monophasic behavior at 35°C and 100 bar in agreement with the disappearance of the meniscus (Fig. 2e), confirmed and proved that a single phase mixture is obtained at these conditions.

At a relatively higher temperature of 55°C and 100 bar (Figs. 2b, 2c, and 2d) a biphasic mixture was observed, i.e. presence of meniscus (Figure S11). The bottom part of the reactor, containing the denser phase (liquid phase), and the top part, representing the lighter phase (vapor phase), were separately characterized using spectroscopy (Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d, respectively).

In Fig. 4c, the liquid phase exhibits the assigned bands of TEP, acetone and the Fermi diads of CO2. The presence of the CO2 Fermi diad bands at around 1265 cm−1 and 1370 cm−1 suggests its solubility in the TEP and acetone mixtures in all ratios. Furthermore, the intensity variations of the TEP and acetone bands are directly proportional to the concentration of these species in the mixture [25]. However, the quantification of the composition were not investigated further.

In contrast, Fig. 4d, shows the vapor phase where the bands of TEP at 1442 cm−1 and 1477 cm−1 and the band of acetone at 1690–1740 cm−1 were absent. This absence indicates that acetone and TEP are not solubilized in the vapor phase and remained in the liquid phase under the applied pressure, which is very important in the delamination process, as TEP is the active solvent dissolving the PVDF [7]. The TEP in liquid state especially at heated condition dissolves the PVDF binder. This means that for the delamination process, maximum TEP content is used. Although the solubility of CO2-acetone at 55°C and 100 bar is known, the role of TEP in this ternary mixture should not be overlooked. Besides, if acetone is present in the vapor phase, its concentration (<0.05) is low to be detected due to the resolution of the spectrometer used. Furthermore, TEP is less likely to migrate to the vapor phase compared to acetone, as TEP has a lower solubility in CO2 and a much higher boiling point (Tb = 211°C) (see Figure S11).

Aside from the presence of the bands of CO2 in the liquid phase, the determination of its solubility requires further investigation. In this regard, an exhaustive analysis of the Fermi diads was conducted to confirm the CO2 solubility in the liquid phase, particularly in the biphasic region. The Fermi diad peaks of CO2 in all ternary mixtures under monophasic, liquid, and vapor conditions are presented in Fig. 5. The binary phase diagrams of N2 with either TEP or acetone indicate a minimal solubility of N2 in these solvents (Figure S12). This limited solubility is also reflected in the Raman spectroscopy data presented in Figure S13. Unlike the observations with CO2, the Raman peak of N2 at wavenumbers above 2300 cm−1 exhibits significantly lower intensity, particularly in the liquid phase. This observation is consistent with the data presented in Table 3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of temperature on the solubility of CO2 and N2 in the Acetone- TEP mixture
ce:section.ce:para: Table 2 suggests that the amount of solubilized CO2 in the TEP-acetone mixtures varies depending on their initial compositions as shown by the height of the meniscus, which is not true for N2. To complement experimental observation and in-situ spectroscopy, we used the PR-EOS by considering the binary interaction parameters equal to 0 [30–34], to determine the ternary diagram for different temperatures in order to analyze its influence on the solubility of CO2 and N2 in the liquid phase (see Fig. 6).

Fig. 6 presents the phase diagrams highlighting the bubble curves of either CO2 or N2 with TEP and acetone, it can be inferred that the behavior of the mixture with CO2 varies with temperature, such that the solubility of the CO2 to the TEP-acetone is also temperature dependent; with higher solubility at lower temperature at isobaric conditions. Overall, the trend of the data follows that a decrease in the mixture’s density is observed with increase of temperature. While the same trend for solubility data is observed such that a decrease of CO2 amount decreases with increase of temperature. This could explain the physical observation of the meniscus behavior.

There is an interesting variation of the solubility depending on the ratio between acetone with TEP and CO2. It is worth noticing that at 35°C, the ternary mixture of CO2-TEP-Acetone achieves a single phase system, where a monophasic mixture is observed (Fig. 2e and Figs. S1–5). On the other hand, the behavior of N2-mixed ternary system is almost stagnant, the minimal shift of the bubble curve suggests that the solubility of N2 is limited to around 10 % mol fraction in all mixtures and in all temperatures investigated (Fig. 6b). The behavior of the fluid as derived from the PR-EOS is almost the same to the physical observation on the movement of meniscus, such that a constant meniscus height value is obtained from N2 mixtures at 100 bar in all temperature (Table 3) across various TEP-acetone ratios. Unlike CO2, the biphasic region for the ternary mixture of TEP-Acetone with N2, is more extensive and this remained constant at 100 bar even with varied temperatures. Hence, in this case, temperature has virtually no effect in the system, in the explored range. This could mean that the system is so stable that any parameter does not alter the solubility of N2 in the TEP-acetone nor the behavior of the mixture, at least in the studied conditions.

Although Fig. 6 can somehow explain the trends and other observed data during the conduct of this experiment, an experimental uncertainty has to be considered such that, the figure presents only an approximation of the bubble curves and could only be used as a guide for discussion. This is because, TEP is a non-referenced material, meaning there is not a lot of data available to compare the values obtained, this is especially true in ternary system with CO2 (N2) and acetone, and the calculation using the PR-EOS requiring the interaction parameter (kij) which was not determined and was directly set to zero.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of temperature, CO2 (N2) and acetone on the viscosity of TEP
ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of CO2/N2 in the liquid phase is then used to calculate the viscosity. The saturated liquid viscosity of TEP at different temperatures was obtained from a fitting estimation provided by Prosim [16] then the effect of the high pressure on liquid TEP viscosity is considered from Lucas’ equation [19] and lastly, the viscosity of the mixture with the composition using the Grunberg and Nissan (1949) formula, these are all detailed in the supporting information. Fig. 7 (and Table S1) presents the comparison of the impact of CO2 against N2 over the viscosity of TEP.

In Fig. 7a, the viscosity of the binary mixture of CO2 and TEP is extremely low compared to that of N2-TEP in the temperature range presented. Indicating that more molecules of CO2 solubilized into the liquid phase compared to N2, resulting to the dilution effect on the viscosity. This shows that the solubility limit of CO2 is higher than to N2 with TEP. Comparing the molar volume of CO2 and N2, CO2 is greater in all temperatures at 100 bar; 14.8 kmol/m3, 5.7 kmol/m3, 4.3 kmol/m3, and 3.8 kmol/m3 at 35°C, 70°C, 100°C, and 120°C, respectively while N2 has 3.9 kmol/m3,3.5 kmol/m3, 3.2 kmol/m3, and 3.0 kmol/m3 at the same temperature and pressure conditions. Despite the fact that N2 is less dense than CO2 at these conditions, there was 4–8 fold more molecules of solubilized CO2 (see Fig. 6 and Table S1) added to TEP in the liquid phase than N2. Also, the N2-TEP viscosity data follows the curve shape of pure TEP because there is only a very small amount of N2 solubilized to TEP whatever the considered temperature, which means that at these conditions, N2 cannot reduce the viscosity of TEP further and would require higher pressure at much lower temperature to increase the solubility of N2. In other words, since there is a low molar fraction of N2 in the binary system with TEP, the vapor pressure of TEP controls the solubility and viscosity of the system. In general, the viscosity of a mixture is lowered down once a less dense fluid is added to a fluid, in this case CO2 and N2 are less dense than TEP and so their addition subsequently reduced the viscosity.

It is clear that the addition of acetone has the same purpose to that of the CO2 and N2, i.e.: to lower down the viscosity of TEP. In Figs. 7b and 7c, it is logical that the addition of acetone in the ternary system created a synergistic effect with CO2 on lowering down the viscosity of TEP. This synergistic effect is clearly indicated by the data on the height of meniscus (Table 2) such that increasing the amount of acetone, increases the height of meniscus and therefore the solubilized CO2 as discussed above. This means that the lowering down of the viscosity of TEP is mainly due to CO2 rather than acetone for the CO2 system. Acetone as light organic compound is usually used as a cosolvent for CO2 because it can enhance both the solvating power and the polarity of CO2 [11]. In general, the reduction of viscosity of TEP is due to the addition of less dense molecule such as the acetone, N2, and CO2.

The benefits of the use of pressurized fluids for recycling processes may extend beyond just the use of CO2, particularly in terms of solvent consumption. In a stirred setup with a solvent consumption ratio of 10:1 [7], the pressurized system using N2 reduced the ratio to 9.3:1. In contrast, working with CO2 brought it down significantly to < 1.5:1. This innovative process resulted in a 6.7-fold reduction in solvent consumption compared to state-of-the-art processes for the delamination of LIB positive electrodes. Therefore, CO2 plays a crucial role in the delamination process, enabling significant advantages such as eliminating the need for a stirring mechanism, reducing solvent consumption, maintaining mild temperature conditions, and achieving faster processing times. In addition to these benefits, CO₂ selectively dissolves PVDF among all the components of the positive electrode. This selective dissolution maintains the integrity of the other components, allowing them to be reused without further treatments. It also enables the separation and recycling of all components without damage, preserving their characteristics. This makes CO2-based recycling process a highly advanced technology, setting a higher standard and elevating the state of recycling compared to other similar processes.

The investigation to understand the purpose of the addition of acetone and CO2 in the delamination of positive electrode was clarified in this study. It revealed that CO2 significantly enhances delamination efficiency, contributing an additional 35 % efficiency. Consequently, this necessitated an examination of fluid behavior under high pressure and high temperature conditions.

New experimental data were presented to estimate the solubility and viscosity of mixtures consisting of TEP, acetone, and CO2/N2 at 100 bar across various temperatures. Physical observations indicated the presence of a biphasic system, as suggested by the appearance of a meniscus. Raman spectroscopy confirmed that CO2 was present in both phases: the vapor phase (or light phase) contained only CO2, while the liquid phase (or dense phase) contained TEP, acetone, and CO2. The solubilized CO2 was correlated with the observed movement of the meniscus to higher levels. Additionally, the study showed that acetone increases CO2 solubility, causing the meniscus to rise with increasing acetone concentration. Acetone and CO2 together exhibited a synergistic effect in reducing the viscosity of TEP.
In contrast, the use of N2 in the delamination of multi-materials, such as the positive electrode of a LIB, showed no significant impact in this study.

These new observations will advance scientific understanding, particularly in fields like recycling, where the application of pressurized fluids can offer new insights and improve state-of-the-art technologies. This data can be used to optimize operational parameters for the recycling of positive electrodes in LIBs using pressurized fluids, a topic currently gaining attention.

Hayagan Neil: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Guillou Pierre: Writing – review & editing, Data curation. Olchowka Jacob: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Ercicek Fatma: Methodology. Lecoutre Carole: Methodology. Nguyen Olivier: Methodology. Aymonier Cyril: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Marre Samuel: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Erriguible Arnaud: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization. Philippot Gilles: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: As part of the DESTINY PhD Programme, the authors acknowledge the funding from the European Union's Horizon2020 research and innovation programme under the Marie Skłodowska-Curie Actions COFUND (Grant Agreement #945357), co-funded by Region Nouvelle Aquitaine (Project Region AAPR2021A-2020-11998810), from ANR through the STORE-EX Labex project ANR-10-LABX-76-01, and the REGENERATE JCJC project ANR-22-CE05-0004. This study also received financial support from the French government in the framework of the University of Bordeaux's IdEx "Investments for the Future" program / GPR PPM. We also acknowledge the CNRS.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jcou.2025.103080.
